subject: [PATCH] ACPI: NUMA: Only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on

reviews.author: Huang, Kai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer asked if other modules besides CXL_ACPI use the CFMWS information for CXL memory regions, probing the implications of only parsing CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is enabled

Reviewer noted that the patch only avoids detecting CFMWS, which doesn't provide any NUMA information, and therefore kernel assigns a 'faked' NUMA node for each of them, not actually losing any NUMA info.
sentiment_signals: no clear signal from reviewer's comment
reviews.raw_body:
Besides CXL_ACPI, is there any other module(s) that uses this information
for these CXL memory regions?

---

The normal NUMA info described in SRAT is still there.  It only avoids
detecting CFMWS, which doesn't provide any NUMA info actually -- that's why
kernel assigns a 'faked' NUMA node for each of them.

So we are not losing anything AFAICT.

reviews.author: Huang, Kai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Huang, Kai questioned the 'strange' behavior of the patch and provided ACPI table dumps to demonstrate that CEDT tables are present on the platform, suggesting that the issue may not be as described in the patch.

Reviewer noted that the patch's logic to only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on causes a problem where the NUMA structure is lost even though it's accurately described by BIOS, and suggested re-evaluating whether this issue should be handled in the acpi code or if lazy-creating these things would be a better approach.

Reviewer questioned the rationality of permanently losing NUMA structure information when CXL_ACPI is built, suggesting it might be better to lazy-create these entries instead

Reviewer noted that the patch's decision to only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on incurs a cost if CXL_ACPI is not compiled out by default, and requested justification for this design choice.
reviews.raw_body:
How to check whether it is "strange"?

I dumped the ACPI table on that platform, and what I can see is there's CEDT
table which contains couple of "[CXL Host Bridge Structure]" and "[CXL Fixed
Memory Window Structure]".

I pasted one for each of them here FYI:

[024h 0036 001h]               Subtable Type : 00 [CXL Host Bridge
Structure]
[025h 0037 001h]                    Reserved : 00
[026h 0038 002h]                      Length : 0020
[028h 0040 004h]      Associated host bridge : 00000003
[02Ch 0044 004h]       Specification version : 00000001
[030h 0048 004h]                    Reserved : 00000000
[034h 0052 008h]               Register base : 00000000997F0000
[03Ch 0060 008h]             Register length : 0000000000010000

[124h 0292 001h]               Subtable Type : 01 [CXL Fixed Memory Window
Structure]
[125h 0293 001h]                    Reserved : 00
[126h 0294 002h]                      Length : 0034
[128h 0296 004h]                    Reserved : 00000000
[12Ch 0300 008h]         Window base address : 0000002080000000
[134h 0308 008h]                 Window size : 0000017B00000000
[13Ch 0316 001h]    Interleave Members (2^n) : 02
[13Dh 0317 001h]       Interleave Arithmetic : 01
[13Eh 0318 002h]                    Reserved : 0000
[140h 0320 004h]                 Granularity : 00000000
[144h 0324 002h]                Restrictions : 0006
[146h 0326 002h]                       QtgId : 0000
[148h 0328 004h]                First Target : 00000003
[14Ch 0332 004h]                 Next Target : 00000023
[150h 0336 004h]                 Next Target : 00000043
[154h 0340 004h]                 Next Target : 00000053

---

My machine has a legitimate SRAT.  The kernel detects it has 4 nodes with
256 CPUs + 256G memory.

---

I don't know how to check whether the BIOS "should" or "should not" generate
CEDT.  I can check my machine if you let me know what to check specifically?

---

Yeah, and it is due to "there's a cost" if we don't compile out by default.

Hope that justifies?

reviews.author: Huang, Kai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer agreed that the patch is simpler, but suggested keeping Gregory's comment to provide additional information if needed.

Reviewer Huang, Kai expressed concern that disabling CXL_ACPI might not be feasible for some users due to BIOS limitations and suggested the patch is still worth implementing despite this issue.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah agreed this is simpler.  Thanks for the suggestion.

I would like to honor Gregory's suggestion about the comment since it
provides more info if you agree?

(I also added "impacts certain NUMA userspace ABIs")

/*
 * Some platforms report CEDT CFMWS for hotplug device support. These
 * windows are unusable without CXL drivers, so don't reserve fake nodes
 * if they're compiled out - it wastes memory on per-node structures and
 * impacts certain NUMA userspace ABIs.
 */

---

Sure I can (I need to figure out how, though).  But I think it's still
useful for developers because some of them will tend to only enable Kconfig
options that they need to save build time.

And sometimes it's not quite easy to turn off CXL in the BIOS since the
machine would be mostly remote.

So I think this patch is still worthy to do?

2026-03-05.patch_summary: This patch modifies the ACPI NUMA code to only parse CFMWS (Compact Firmware Memory Window Specification) at boot when CXL_ACPI is enabled. This change prevents the loss of NUMA structure information, which would otherwise be lost if CXL_ACPI is built into the kernel. The approach taken is to conditionally create these structures based on the presence of CXL_ACPI.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author is addressing concerns about the patch's impact on existing systems and potential firmware behavior, explaining that he wants to understand if there's 'odd firmware behavior' before making changes.
reviews.raw_body:
Well, I'm mostly confused why there are CEDT entries for hardware that
presumably isn't even there - unless this platform is reserving space
for future hotplug.  Just want to make sure we're not adjusting for
strange firmware behavior.

The only platform we've seen this behavior on previously was QEMU, but
that was because it never emitted SRAT, so i was wondering if there's
odd firmware behavior going on (emitting CEDTs when it shouldn't) before
we jump to dropping nodes that would have otherwise have been present
on existing systems who might have compiled CXL_ACPI out.

You are taking something away by nature of compiling something out by
default that was previously not compiled out by default.

~Gregory

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledges reviewer's point about potential platform issue, but doesn't necessarily disagree with the change and suggests it's worth understanding whether it's a Linux or platform problem.

Author acknowledged the reviewer's concern that the patch prevents loading modules that use CEDT entries when CXL_ACPI is not built, and agreed to add a comment to clarify this in acpi_numa_init_cxl.
reviews.raw_body:
Sorry, i don't mean to come off rough - I don't even necessarily
disagree with the change here, but it is worth taking a moment to
understand whether this is actually a Linux issue or an platform issue.

Up until now there's been some assumption that if a device is attached
there will be CEDT windows - but this would be the first time i've seen
a platform throw up CEDT windows for devices that don't exist.

Anyway, this is good information for Linux to know and worth a comment.
We don't know what other people have built that depend on the current
behavior.

---

If that's the case then cool :]

May be worth a comment in acpi_numa_init_cxl - something like:

/*
 * Some platforms report CEDT CFMWS for hotplug device support. These
 * windows are unusable without CXL drivers, so don't reserve fake nodes
 * if they're compiled out - it wastes memory on per-node structures.
 */

Reviewed-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

~Gregory

reviews.author: dan.j.williams
reviews.summary:
Reviewer dan.j.williams noted that CXL hotplug windows are expected to be populated in the future, and SRAT cannot make claims about future CXL hotplug, so Linux should reserve some numa nodes for dynamic affinity determination using HMAT Generic Port and device CDAT information.

Reviewer dan.j.williams suggested refactoring the patch to use a conditional compilation approach instead of adding ifdefs, allowing the acpi_table_parse_cedt() call to be skipped when CONFIG_CXL_ACPI is disabled.
reviews.raw_body:
So these are fine. These are CXL hotplug windows and the expectation is
that they *might* be populated in the future. SRAT can not make claims
about future CXL hotplug (see NOTE). So the expecation for CXL hotplug
is reserve some numa nodes that Linux can determine the affinity of
dynamically with HMAT Generic Port and device CDAT information.

NOTE: SRAT *does* make claims about the affinity of future *ACPI*
Hotplug, but in that case the platform statically knows something about
the configuration of memory that can possibly be plugged in the future.

---

I think it makes sense that if you disable CXL hotplug by setting
CONFIG_CXL_ACPI=n then no need to reserve numa ids. However, just do
something like this rather than add ifdefs to the code:

diff --git a/drivers/acpi/numa/srat.c b/drivers/acpi/numa/srat.c
index aa87ee1583a4..62d4a8df0b8c 100644
--- a/drivers/acpi/numa/srat.c
+++ b/drivers/acpi/numa/srat.c
@@ -654,8 +654,11 @@ int __init acpi_numa_init(void)
 	}
 	last_real_pxm = fake_pxm;
 	fake_pxm++;
-	acpi_table_parse_cedt(ACPI_CEDT_TYPE_CFMWS, acpi_parse_cfmws,
-			      &fake_pxm);
+
+	/* No need to expand numa nodes if CXL is disabled */
+	if (IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_CXL_ACPI))
+		acpi_table_parse_cedt(ACPI_CEDT_TYPE_CFMWS, acpi_parse_cfmws,
+				      &fake_pxm);
 
 	if (cnt < 0)
 		return cnt;

The call to acpi_table_parse_cedt() will get skipped and the code for
acpi_parse_cfmws() will get automatically compiled out of the file.

At the same time I doubt this patch provides end users much value in
practice as most distro kernels have CONFIG_CXL_ACPI, and the few end
users that have CXL are not going blink at the overhead to support the
full feature set.

Can you not just disable CXL support in the BIOS for your system and
avoid complicating this code path for a small win?

reviews.author: dan.j.williams
reviews.summary:
reviewer questioned the design decision to only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on, pointing out that this would prevent loading modules that rely on CEDT entries even if they are built into the kernel

Reviewer dan.j.williams expressed concern that the patch's complexity may be excessive for a rare edge case, suggesting that a simpler solution using /* No need to expand numa nodes if CXL is disabled */ would suffice.

Reviewer dan.j.williams expressed dissatisfaction with the inclusion of a lengthy paragraph in the patch description, deeming it unnecessary and preferring concise code changes.
reviews.raw_body:
Huang, Kai wrote:
[..]

---

I feel like this is just too much scary detail for a tiny corner case
that maybe only effects a handful of kernel developers at hardware
companies with advanced server platforms.

    /* No need to expand numa nodes if CXL is disabled */

...is more than sufficient.

---

A small 2 line patch to save a few megabytes of memory, sure. A
paragraph of context that almost nobody will ever care about on a go
forward basis? No, thank you.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author agreed to add a note about the limitation of CXL_ACPI being built out to the patch's changelog, indicating no further action is needed.
reviews.raw_body:
eh throw it in the changelog *shrug*, i think it's useful context.

~Gregory

2026-03-04.patch_summary: This patch modifies the ACPI NUMA code to only parse CFMWS (Compact Firmware Memory Window Specification) at boot when CXL_ACPI is enabled. This change prevents the loss of NUMA structure information, which would otherwise be lost if CXL_ACPI is built into the kernel. The approach taken is to conditionally create these structures based on the presence of CXL_ACPI.
